patch.value: Inventory the CLI-to-core call graph: map every command to core modules and classify logic for SDK promotion

Produce the definitive map: for each of the ~60 commands, which src/core functions it calls, and which logic currently living in src/cli/commands (parsing aside) is business logic that must move behind the SDK. Output feeds both pm-usfg promotion tasks and pm-n7rr consolidation clusters. Duplicate check: no existing call-graph/inventory item.

Checked-in inventory (docs/ or item body) maps every CLI command to core modules + identifies business logic embedded in command handlers; each promotion task (item-lifecycle/query/annotations/governance/schema-config) gets its exact function list; findings recorded as notes on pm-usfg

value.text: Evidence: added docs/SDK_PRIMITIVE_INVENTORY.md as the checked-in SDK-first command-family inventory for parent pm-usfg. It records the current boundary baseline, maps command families to SDK promotion lanes (lifecycle, query/read, annotations/links, schema/config/profile/init, governance/maintenance, history, plan, execution/diagnostics), and documents the ratchet workflow for reducing private src/core imports as primitives move into src/sdk. Verification passed: pm linked typecheck, full coverage 263 files / 5411 tests at 100/100/100/100, pnpm lint, docs/skills gate, and isolated temp-project dogfood.

patch.value: Added docs/SDK_PRIMITIVE_INVENTORY.md with a checked-in CLI/MCP-to-SDK primitive inventory, current import-boundary baseline, command-family promotion lanes, and ratchet workflow for moving private core access behind SDK primitives.
Agents have an explicit, low-context inventory for SDK-first promotion work and can choose future primitive lanes without re-discovering the current CLI/MCP core coupling.
Inventory document now maps command families to SDK promotion lanes and records the current 101-file boundary scan / 857-edge private core baseline used by the static ratchet gate.
Completed SDK-first command-family inventory with current boundary baseline and promotion-lane mapping.
